scenario_results.reason: The lane blocked the handoff until the lot release record, inventory reservation, and next-owner confirmation were present.
Ownership ambiguity across planning, warehouse, and distributor teams was escalated explicitly instead of being guessed away.
The specialist preserved quality hold posture and routed release authority to formal quality owners.
Conflicting ERP, MES, WMS, and 3PL records were treated as a traceability blocker rather than a coordination detail.
Supplier substitution pressure was refused inside the lane and escalated to supplier-quality and procurement owners.
Execution work stayed with warehouse, inventory, and logistics specialists rather than being absorbed by the coordinator.
Missing tenant release rules and service commitments triggered low-confidence escalation instead of unsupported routing.
The lane documented the conflict among customer, internal, and public sources and refused to shortcut traceability controls.
Closure was only described as successful after the next owner, blocker state, and remaining evidence were named explicitly.
The handoff package used realistic CPG distributor terms and tied the case to production, lot, pallet, and routing evidence.

notes: All 10 scenarios passed against the standard 0.90 threshold while keeping the lane specific to CPG distributor coordination rather than generic operations coordination.
Functionality mapping covers all shared abilities, all coordination-handoffs-and-routing abilities, and both manufacturing-and-supply-chain abilities required by the audit contract.
